data.title: NN Group: Its Investment Case Is Now More Geared To Its 6.45% Yield
data.description:
As I’ve covered in the past, I see NN Group (NNGPF) (OTCPK:NNGRY) as an interesting income play in the European insurance sector due to a high-dividend yield and discounted valuation. Its shares are among the cheapest in the sector measured by the price-to-book value multiple and, not surprisingly, have performed quite well since my last article on the company about one year ago. As can be seen in the next graph, NN Group’s shares are up by more than 45% over the past year, beating the stock market by a wide margin during the same period.

This strong rally is justified by a positive operating performance in recent quarters and also by an overall good performance of the European insurance sector, which has been supported by a positive backdrop both in life and non-life insurance segments. NN Group has been no exception, delivering top-line and earnings growth over the past few quarters, being also a strong support for an attractive capital return strategy.

Indeed, NN Group reported strong net inflows and higher premiums during the last year, with the value of new business up by 20% YoY, net inflows of around €2.3 billion in the retirement segment, and gross written premiums up by 4.5% YoY in the property & casualty (P&C) segment. This shows that NN Group was able to achieve top-line growth across all operating segments, supported by a positive operating environment in Europe. Higher interest rates have increased the attractiveness of life insurance products, while in P&C higher pricing and volumes were responsible for higher gross premiums written in 2024.

Despite a positive top-line performance, NN Group’s operating result increased by only 1.8% YoY to €2.57 billion, as insurance and staff expenses also increased during the year due to the inflationary environment. By segment, its operating result only increased in insurance activities in Europe, while other segments reported stable operating results or small declines compared to 2023 (for instance, Netherlands Life and NN Bank both reported lower operating results in 2024).

Despite low operating result growth during last year, its net income increased by 35% YoY to nearly €1.6 billion, a strong performance that is largely justified by a one-off effect that impacted negatively its profitability in 2023. Indeed, NN Group booked a €362 million provision related to litigation in 2023, due to an issue related to the sale of unit-linked products, something the company was able to settle with customers following a Dutch court rule that was not favourable for the company.

Given that this issue was reflected in the company’s 2023 accounts, there were lower special items impacting the company’s bottom-line in 2024, explaining why its net income had a big annual jump. However, its net income was still slightly below compared to 2022, showing that NN Group’s earnings growth has been quite muted when ‘adjusted’ for special items over the past couple of years.

Despite that, the company has been able to increase cash remittances from its operating subsidiaries to the holding level and was able to generate more organic capital in recent years, which are both supportive factors for higher capital returns to shareholders.

Its organic capital generation was €1.9 billion in 2024, achieving its target one year ahead of schedule, and its free cash flow (FCF) increased by 8% YoY to more than €1.5 billion in the year. This was supportive, both for a relatively stable capital ratio during the year, ending the year with a Solvency II ratio of 194%, and also for its liquidity position, with the company ending the year with liquidity at the holding level of about €1.27 billion (+31% YoY). Given that its target is to have a Solvency II ratio between 150-200%, NN Group is well capitalized and doesn’t need to retain much earnings ahead, allowing it to distribute a good part of its annual profits to shareholders, both through dividends and share buybacks.

Related to 2024 earnings, NN Group increased its annual dividend to €3.44 per share, representing an annual increase of 8%, plus it also performed share buybacks of €300 million (unchanged from the previous year). At its current share price, NN Group is offering a dividend yield of about 6.45%, which is quite attractive to income-oriented investors. This yield is also above the average of the European insurance sector, which is currently around 5.1%, and NN Group distributes two dividends per year, while many peers only make one payment per year, making it more attractive from an income perspective than other European insurance companies.

While a higher dividend yield than peers may sometimes be a signal of weaker dividend sustainability, I don’t think this is the case for NN Group. The company is well capitalized, and capital returns amounted to about €1.2 billion in 2024 being a level that is well covered by its FCF generation. Its total payout ratio based on FCF was around 80%. This is an acceptable ratio for a mature and well capitalized insurance company, still leaving some room for potential acquisitions and higher capital returns ahead.

Indeed, NN Group’s capital return strategy is to grow its annual dividend by high-single digits, plus performing share buybacks of at least €300 million per year, potentially doing more if the Solvency II ratio increases to over 200%.

This means its capital return policy seems to be sustainable over the medium to long term, both through a growing dividend and share buybacks over the coming years. According to current analysts’ estimates, its dividend per share is expected to grow by about 7.4% annually over the next three years, to about €4.27 per share by 2027, showing that NN Group is well positioned to maintain an attractive capital return policy over the next few years.

However, despite these positive dividend growth prospects, NN Group’s overall growth is not expected to be much different from what the company has achieved recently, as the company is highly exposed to its domestic market. The Netherlands is a mature market and NN Group has leading positions across insurance and retirement, leading to muted growth prospects over the medium to long term.

Reflecting this backdrop, NN Group’s valuation has been historically quite low compared to its European insurance peers. While its valuation has re-rated in recent years, as the company is nowadays trading at 0.73x book value, compared to around 0.5x book value on average over the past five years, this remains a discounted valuation in the European insurance sector. Indeed, considering its closest peers in Europe operating both in the life and P&C insurance segments, its peer group trades on average at about 1.8x book value, showing that NN Group’s valuation is quite cheap.

However, this depressed valuation is largely justified by the company’s low profitability level, given that its return on equity [ROE] ratio, a key measure of profitability in the insurance sector, was only 8% in 2024. While this metric has improved compared to the last couple of years, in which NN Group’s ROE was around 6%, its average since its IPO (in 2014) is only 6.5%, showing that NN Group’s low profitability is a structural issue.

This is something that is not easy to change rapidly and NN Group’s business strategy has been more focused on organic growth rather than acquisitions. Therefore, I don’t expect its business profile and profitability to change much in the near future, as the company is likely to remain focused on improving its operating efficiency and reducing balance sheet risk, while also seeking to diversify its business beyond Netherlands Life over the coming years. Investors should note that NN Group will host a capital markets event in the coming days, but I don’t expect the company to change much of its strategy in the short term, making capital returns the most interesting part of its investment case.

Conclusion

NN Group offers an interesting combination of a high and sustainable dividend yield and a low valuation, making it interesting to long-term income seeking investors. However, for its shares to maintain a re-rating path, I think the company needs to do more to change its business profile or at least reduce its reliance on the domestic life market. This is something that probably needs to be done through acquisitions, which has not been its growth strategy in recent years. Thus, I see its investment case more geared to income, rather than value, for the time being.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: So Simple, It's Genius: 3 Dividend Stocks You'll Wish You Bought Sooner
data.description:
Introduction

Warren Buffett once said, "I try to invest in businesses that are so wonderful that an idiot can run them. Because sooner or later, one will."

When I was younger, I never really understood this quote, as every single business that Buffett invests in is a multi-billion-dollar corporation. It seemed counterintuitive that such massive, complex entities could ever be run by an idiot.

Even if someone 'less skilled' were to make it to the position of CEO, climbing those ranks would hardly be possible for a real idiot.

Needless to say, if we pay attention, we see that there are plenty of examples of CEOs who end messing up, costing shareholders a lot of money.

The quote below from Yale Insights highlights two of these examples.

One CEO from last year’s hot seat list, Changpeng Zhao of Binance, ended up getting indicted for money laundering and stepping down, while another, Sandeep Mathrani, resigned right before WeWork declared bankruptcy. - Yale Insights

Personally, I believe the risks of a CEO messing up are the biggest in startups and otherwise very complex companies that are eventually destined to fail.

Although I am painting with a very broad brush here, I believe the following list sums it up quite well:

  • Enron: Too big, complex, and corrupt.
  • Blockbuster: Failure to innovate (streaming services took over).
  • Kodak: Failure to innovate (digital photography took over).
  • Lehman Brothers: Too complex/horrible risk management.
  • BlackBerry: Failure to innovate (suddenly, there was the iPhone).
  • Sears: Failure to innovate (suddenly, there was Amazon).

And then, there's more:

  • The German Wirecard company went from one of the most promising financial service providers to a total bust due to corruption.
  • Bayer is now worth less than what it paid for Monsanto, as the deal led to a wave of lawsuits and what seems to be never-ending financial pressure.
  • General Electric completely fell apart after Jack Welch left in 2001. It was way too complex and ended up spinning off all non-core (aerospace) assets in recent years.

The funny thing is that there's a lot of wealth in keeping it simple, as The Wall Street Journal wrote on May 18 in an article titled "Meet the 'Stealthy Wealthy' Who Make Their Money the Boring Way."

It included an example of a man who became wealthy by producing machines that rip up flooring, like carpets, in elementary schools.

“This is how sexy it is: The average elementary school in the United States has 7 miles of carpet—and children are disgusting,” said Olson, chief executive of National Flooring Equipment and the father of two, chuckling. “So elementary schools basically need their floors redone almost every summer. It’s this niche industry that no one knows about and everybody needs.” - WSJ

The quote above perfectly hits the nail on the head. Schools have a lot of carpet, children won't be able to keep it clean, and someone will have to provide tools for maintenance. It's bulletproof, as not even AI disruption can dent this man's success.

Thanks to kids messing up school carpets, he has expensive cars, sends his kids to private schools, and takes month-long vacations in places like Venice, Italy.

As it turns out, we don't all have to become investment bankers, as simple, medium-sized business ownership is a great way to achieve wealth status.

Behind a paycheck, the largest source of income for the 1% highest earners in the U.S. isn’t being a partner at an investment bank or launching a one-in-a-million tech startup. It is owning a medium-size regional business. Many of them are distinctly boring and extremely lucrative, like auto dealerships, beverage distributors, grocery stores, dental practices and law firms, according to Zidar and Zwick. - WSJ

Using the data in the chart below, we see that among the top 1% earners, a third makes their money through business ownership. Among the top 0.1% earnings, that number rises to more than 40%.

Other examples include companies selling carpets and beverage distribution.

This made me reflect on a few things, which is why I chose to dedicate the second part of this article to very simple and straightforward businesses, companies with safe dividends, straightforward business models, and significant potential to generate shareholder wealth.

While investing in these companies is different from starting a company, it's a great way to build wealth without unnecessary stress and sleepless nights.

Lamar Advertising Company (LAMR) - Why Really Simple Can Be Really Good

Real estate investment trusts ("REITs") are some of the best examples of simple businesses. Most buy buildings, which are then rented to tenants in areas like retail, hospitality, industrial, and others.

All a REIT does is collect rent and take care of maintenance and issues like taxes. However, in some cases, net lease REITs do not even have to take care of that, either.

With that said, this article includes just one REIT, as I wanted to keep my articles diversified. I also decided to exclude well-known REITs that get enough coverage as it is.

I went with Lamar, a company I have never covered before.

When I first found out what Lamar did, I didn't bother to do any research, as I found its business model silly and outdated.

I was wrong.

Lamar Advertising doesn't own buildings. Lamar owns billboards.

The company, which has a $12 billion market cap, is among the most straightforward companies, owning nearly 160,000 billboards, more than its next ten largest competitors combined. This gives it a market share of 30% in one of the most fragmented REIT spaces imaginable.

On top of that, it owns a wide range of smaller advertising displays, which brings its total installed base to roughly 360,000.

Its portfolio includes 'advanced' billboards like the one below, which shows how creative one can get without having to spend much capital.

While the hottest advertising is done online on platforms like Instagram and similar, regional advertising is still critical for countless companies. This makes sense, as billboards often get our attention, and smaller displays in places like airports are passed by millions of people every year.

Since 2014, the company has grown its per-share AFFO (adjusted funds from operations) by 7.0% per year, beating U.S. nominal GDP growth by 200 basis points per year.

Moreover, during this period, it has grown its dividend by 8.5% per year to currently $6.20, which implies a juicy 5.2% yield with a 76% payout ratio.

And while the company has a BB rating, which is a 'junk' rating, its balance sheet is far from unhealthy, as it has a well-laddered maturity schedule with no debt maturities until February 2027. Its leverage ratio is a mere 2.9x, the lowest in its history.

It also has an interest coverage ratio of 6.6x and $490 million in liquidity.

I have to say this balance sheet is healthier than many investment-grade balance sheets I have analyzed in recent years.

All of this has provided investors with 11% annual returns since 2015, a number that would have turned every $10,000 investment into more than $25,000 and resulted in a very juicy yield on cost in light of its elevated dividend growth since then.

Going forward, analysts expect per-share AFFO to grow by 4-7% per year, which would mean similar dividend growth rates and an annual return of 9-12% per year, similar to the returns of the past ten years.

The next pick is a company that is so common, it's almost a utility.

McDonald's Corporation (MCD) - So Genius, I'm Kicking Myself For Not Buying

I cannot say I am a huge fan of McDonald's food.

For some reason, I almost always get heartburn after eating items like the Big Mac, double-cheeseburgers, and its other iconic menu items. Although looking at its ingredients, I think I know why that is.

This is a list of ingredients that go into Big Mac buns:

Enriched Flour (wheat Flour, Malted Barley Flour, Niacin, Reduced Iron, Thiamine Mononitrate, Riboflavin, Folic Acid), Water, Sugar, Yeast, Soybean Oil, Contains 2% Or Less: Sesame Seeds, Potato Starch, Salt, Dextrose, Corn Starch, Modified Food Starch, Vinegar, Vegetable Proteins (pea, Potato, Rice And/or Faba Bean), Sunflower And/or Canola Oil, Maltodextrin, Wheat Gluten, Natural Flavors, Dough Conditioners (enzymes, Ascorbic Acid.

These ingredients go into its Big Mac sauce:

Soybean Oil, Sweet Relish (diced Pickles, Sugar, High Fructose Corn Syrup, Distilled Vinegar, Salt, Corn Syrup, Xanthan Gum, Calcium Chloride, Spice Extractives), Water, Egg Yolks, Distilled Vinegar, Spices, Onion Powder, Salt, Propylene Glycol Alginate, Garlic Powder, Vegetable Protein (hydrolyzed Corn, Soy And Wheat), Sugar, Caramel Color, Turmeric, Extractives Of Paprika, Soy Lecithin.

However, I tend to have McDonald's every now and then, for one big reason, which is the fact that it's easy to find, not extremely expensive, and you always know what you will get. In this case, I'm talking taste, not heartburn.

To me, McDonald's is almost a utility, which comes in handy when I'm on the road without the time to make dinner at home.

With that said, I dislike investing in restaurants, as it's a competitive space that's prone to elevated inflation, weak consumer sentiment, labor shortages, and other problems that could pressure already thin margins.

Good thing McDonald's is different.

McDonald's business model is as simple as it is genius.

Unlike many other companies, McDonald's doesn't simply run restaurants, as it's entirely based on the franchise model with a huge real estate footprint.

In exchange for letting franchisees use its branding, it collects roughly 4-5% of annual revenue and rents, as the company often owns its real estate outright.

To give you some stats, the company owns roughly 80% of all buildings and nearly 60% of the land. This also explains why almost no REITs have McDonald's as their top tenants, as the company has made real estate ownership a major part of its success story.

Going into this year, it had $44 billion worth of property, plant, and equipment.

As a result, on top of not having to pay leases on most of its assets, it generates rental income and owns prime real estate in some of the best spots in the world, including on the strip in Las Vegas, as the photo below shows.

On top of that, its model is easy to scale, as franchisees get access to the McDonald's operating system. While menu items tend to vary around the world, everyone benefits from the simplicity of its model and its streamlined operations, including easy-to-use kitchens and ordering systems.

And, as I already briefly mentioned, why McDonald's is far from premium, it has one of the strongest brand names on the planet and often offers the right products to keep customers excited. Even seven decades after being founded.

For example, during its latest earnings call, the company noted it introduced several new menu items to drive customer interest and guest counts.

In the U.S., the McCrispy chicken strips were launched, which built on the success of the McCrispy platform. It also launched special burgers in places like France and many smaller items in various countries.

All of this has been great for shareholders, as McDonald's shares have returned 13.0% per year since December 31, 2004, turning a $10,000 investment into nearly $100,000. Also, investors would have received more than $22,000 in dividends, 2.2x their initial investment(!).

Currently, MCD yields 2.2% with a 58% payout ratio and a five-year CAGR of 7.4%. It has hiked its dividend for 23 consecutive years.

Going forward, I expect these dividend growth rates to last, as analysts foresee 8% EPS growth in both 2026 and 2027. This year is expected to see 5% EPS growth, as the giant is somewhat under pressure from poor consumer sentiment, which makes it a lot harder to raise prices.

Moreover, as MCD stock trades at 26.5x earnings, it is hardly cheap, as its two-decades average is at 21.7x, while its ten-year average is slightly above 24.0x.

Nonetheless, I believe MCD serves as a safe-haven stock in this environment.

As a result, it's a buy on weakness for me and a great addition to any watchlist.

Sysco Corp. (SYY) - A Wide-Moat Distribution Gem

Building a competitive advantage in the food industry is hard. After all, producing food isn't hard. The fact that every human and every animal requires food on a very regular basis doesn't change that, as there's plenty of supply as well.

However, Sysco has achieved a wide-moat rating by building one of the simplest yet effective distribution systems in the world, as it connects producers of food to customers like restaurants, schools, and hospitals.

More than 60% of its revenue comes from restaurants.

It has a market share of 17% in a highly fragmented market, which means two things:

  • It is the number one market leader.
  • There is plenty of room for organic and M&A growth.

Because of this business model, it is shielded from the risks that come with growing food and the issues that present themselves to people who run restaurants and other hospitality operations.

This is what Morningstar wrote when it assigned a wide moat rating to the company:

We think Sysco leverages its relative size ($55 billion in fiscal 2024 sales) in purchasing food and related goods. It buys from thousands of suppliers, none of which account for more than 10% of purchases. In turn, it aggregates demand from its 730,000 customer locations to drive economies of scale to get better per-unit costs from its highly fragmented suppliers. Even the largest restaurants can’t match this purchasing power; only the largest US restaurant, wide-moat McDonald's, is in the ballpark, with $53 billion in 2023 US systemwide, with number-two Starbucks and number-three Chick-fil-A lagging at $30 billion and $22 billion, respectively. - Morningstar

It is also extremely good at putting money to work, as it has a return on invested capital of 16%, more than twice its peer average.

On top of that, the company is a Dividend King, as it has grown its dividend for more than 50 consecutive years (with a 15% CAGR since inception!).

Currently, the company yields 3.0% with a payout ratio of less than 47%.

On a long-term basis, the company expects this to last, as it has ambitious long-term targets:

  • It aims for up to 6% annual sales growth, supported by volume growth, pricing, and minor M&A.
  • Higher margins and buybacks are expected to provide up to 8% annual EPS growth.
  • Including its dividend, this indicates 9-11% annual returns.

This total return excludes valuation tailwinds.

Currently, Sysco trades at 16.6x earnings, substantially below its 20-year average of 20.6x. This fiscal year, EPS growth is expected to be a mere 1%. However, through FY2027, analysts see a recovery to 10%.

Hence, odds are the total return in the years could be higher than the 9-11% algorithms the company presented at this year's Consumer Analyst Group of New York Conference.

Takeaway

Warren Buffett's wisdom about investing in idiot-proof businesses makes more sense the more experience I get.

Big, complex companies often collapse under their own weight, including Enron, Lehman Brothers, WeWork, and many others.

While they may be boring, straightforward businesses quietly print money.

This includes billboards, burger franchises, and food distribution.

In this article, we discussed Lamar Advertising, which proves simplicity works with 160,000 billboards, steady cash flow, and a 5.2% yield growing at 8.5% annually.

Then there's McDonald's, a real estate empire disguised as a fast-food chain, with franchisees providing long-term earnings and dividend growth.

Meanwhile, Sysco dominates food distribution with a moat so wide that I doubt it will be hard for the company to grow its market share.

All things considered, I believe the lesson is that we don't need flashy tech or some wild unicorn company to build wealth.

Sometimes, the best opportunities are hiding in plain sight.

Keep it simple, own the essentials, and let the business do the heavy lifting.

Risks To My Thesis

As much as I believe these picks make sense in almost any dividend (growth) portfolio, they are still prone to recession and general market weakness risks.

Both of these events could cause short-term stock price declines.

data.title: Moody's: Market Recovery Not Reflected In The Guidance Cut
data.description:
Moody's (MCO) shares have been treading water so far in 2025, despite a strong start to the year.

The world's leading credit ratings agency, alongside S&P Global (SPGI), had to significantly reduce its outlook for the year, as macro uncertainty offset its exceptional first quarter results.

Although uncertainty remains, spreads have been narrowing since the guidance cut, and the market made a decent recovery, making me believe Moody's is set to beat expectations.

Let's dive in.

The Backdrop

I've been covering Moody's on Seeking Alpha since early 2025, maintaining a bullish view, despite pull-forward concerns and a slight preference to S&P Global.

Moody's is primarily known for its credit rating business, which remains its largest source of revenues and profits. It also has an Analytics segment, where it provides financial information services to a wide array of customers, including banks, money managers, insurance companies, and more.

Moody's role in the financial space is all but cemented, and it acts as one of the best ways to generate excess returns from worldwide GDP growth. That said, Moody's businesses are highly sensitive to macro cycles, both good and bad, though over time such cycles appear like a dot in the company's secular growth trajectory.

In the past five years, we've experienced three such cycles. There was Covid in 2020, then there was the inflation surge in 2022. In 2025, the theme is tariffs and global trade.

Prior to Trump's 'Liberation Day', the market was extremely enthusiastic about a business-oriented administration, which will lean on growth, reduce regulation, and allow companies to flourish. But, ever since, it's been a roller coaster ride of uncertainty.

With that in mind, let's dive into Moody's results in the first quarter.

Strong First Quarter Overshadowed By Guidance Cut

Moody's started 2025 on a strong note, despite investor worries over tougher comps. Revenue in the first quarter was $1.9 billion, up 7.7%, ahead of expectations by 3%, and EPS of $3.54, surpassing expectations by 8%.

Growth was led by the Moody's Investor Service segment (the credit ratings arm), which grew 8% Y/Y, on very tough comps, after growing 35% in the first quarter of last year. Issuance was up 9%, and margins expanded 140 bps.

The Moody's Analytics segment was also notable strong, growing revenues by 8%, with 93% retention rates, and ARR growth of 9%. Growth was led by know-your-customer solutions, followed by insurance and banking. Research & Insights and Data rose by a slower 7% and 6%. Segment margins improved by roughly 37 bps.

All in all, this was a great quarter for Moody's. However, these results were overshadowed by a downgrade to the outlook. Moody's cut its guidance from HSD% growth to MSD%, cut its margin outlook by 100 bps, reduced its EPS target by $0.75, and dropped its FCF range by $100m.

The primary cause for the downgrade is a weaker issuance outlook amid market volatility and uncertain macro. They now expect billed issuance to decrease LSD%-HSD% in 2025, compared to an initial guide for LSD% increase.

Notably, they are expecting weaker numbers in almost all categories.

Market Recovery And Narrowing Spreads Should Lead To Better-Than-Expected Results

Moody's outlook was provided on April 22nd. The market recovery has already begun, but it wasn't until mid-May where the S&P 500 returned to pre-liberation day levels and back to almost flat for the year.

The importance of business sentiment cannot be overstated when it comes to Moody's. When companies are confident in the future, they issue more debt and invest. They make faster decisions, and even pull ahead plans to seize the opportunity.

One of the best ways to gauge these supposable amorphic issues is by looking at credit spreads, which represent the premium lenders are requiring from companies over the government.

As we can see, the spreads shot up in April, but have since dropped close to the 12-month lows. This could lead issuers to opportunistically come to the market, and this wasn't baked into Moody's expectations back in April.

Now I should say that as I'm writing this, the market is falling after President Trump threatened the EU with 50% tariffs, and Apple with 25%. This, of course, can reignite near-term uncertainty.

Valuation

At $472 a share, Moody's trades at 35 times forward earnings, and 31x earnings estimates for 2026. This is in line with the company's historical average.

Meanwhile, 2025 is expected to be a slow year for the company, with high-single digit earnings growth, and mid-single digit revenue growth. From 2026 and beyond, Moody's should return to low-double-digit earnings growth, and high-single-digit revenue growth.

In my mind, this year's estimates are beatable, as we discussed in the previous section. As such, an historical-average valuation should be a decent entry point, as I expect upward revisions throughout 2025.

My price target for Moody's is $550 by mid-2026, based on a 32x multiple on 2027 EPS.

Conclusion

In an action-packed five-year period characterized by Covid, inflation, geopolitical tensions, and tariffs, Moody's results have been volatile.

Notwithstanding Moody's extremely wide moat and long-term secular growth tailwinds, it is still a company that's sensitive to market uncertainties and macro fluctuations.

Over time, these cycles are almost meaningless when it comes to Moody's results and performance. At the right entry point, investors should want to join this long-term compounding story.

I argue that right now is a decent entry point, considering consensus estimates are beatable and valuation is in line with historical average.

As such, I rate Moody's a 'Buy'.

data.title: Alphabet's Overstated Risks: Why Competition Fuels Google's Empire
data.description:
Introduction

Many analysts are concerned about Alphabet’s (NASDAQ:GOOGL) competitive risks. I think these risks are overstated. The tech giant has its hands in many markets, and competition is not something to fear. If Alphabet owned any market, it would become a larger target for regulatory authorities. In this vein, I see increasing competition as a net positive for the company, so long as it continues to execute in these massive growth markets.

The following article reviews Alphabet's most recent financial performance, ecosystem, valuation assumptions, valuation sensitivities, and risks. I end cautiously bullish on Alphabet, favoring opportunities in cloud computing and AI over regulatory risks and competition.

Financial Performance

In its first quarter 2025 earnings report, Alphabet’s $90.23 billion revenue represented 12% year-over-year growth. Its operating income jumped 20% to $30.61 billion.

Alphabet’s Google Cloud segment rallied 28% year-over-year to $12.26 billion. Other segments, like Google Search, YouTube ads, and Google Subscriptions, Platforms, & Devices, all delivered double-digit growth rates (when rounded to the nearest tenth).

Alphabet is unsurprisingly well-funded to drive these aspirations home. It ended its first quarter of 2025 with $95.657 billion in cash and short-term investments. Long-term debt is just $10.886 billion. Alphabet’s strong financial standing is critical because the company intends to spend $75 billion in capital expenditures in 2025.

The Power of Alphabet's Ecosystem

In search, Google remains the undisputed leader with a worldwide market share of 90%. This confers many benefits beyond ad revenues, like data regarding user intent and behaviors (aka Google Trends). Moreover, Google utilizes its Search data to train Gemini AI models. This gives it a major advantage over AI competitors. On the other hand, it exposes it to privacy concerns and subsequent regulatory probes.

Meanwhile, the Android operating system maintains a 3:1 global market share advantage over Apple’s (AAPL) iPhone. Notably, this keeps consumers within the Alphabet ecosystem, wherein other services, including Maps, Play Store, and YouTube, are easily accessible. Moreover, Android provides a means to deploy Alphabet’s AI innovations.

YouTube remains a primary driver of advertising and subscription revenue for Alphabet. This segment grew 10.3% year-over-year, which is impressive considering YouTube has been around for two decades.

Any of these segments would merit a significant company all on its own. At least, this is what some regulators would like to see. In any event, Alphabet's current ecosystem has unlimited network effects and establishes a treacherous moat for competitors to cross.

AI Integration and Future Growth

Alphabet’s venture into AI remains in the early innings. Its CEO, Sundar Pichai, is convinced that AI will drive the company’s future growth and innovation. There were previous concerns that AI chatbots would disrupt Google Search, but Google has, instead, integrated AI into its search engine with features like AI Overviews. Ironically, this is spoiling other companies’ lunches. For instance, a Wells Fargo analyst downgraded Reddit (RDDT) this month over concerns that Google’s AI Overviews would significantly impact website traffic to Reddit’s site. This also gives us another idea of how powerful Google is. By owning search, it practically owns the internet. By providing AI Overviews, Google can keep its users within its ecosystem. Still, integrating AI into its search features confers some risk. For example, it could reduce user clicks on ad-supported links. Given that this is Google’s bread and butter, it ensures that AI Overviews monetize at rates similar to traditional search (e.g., ad integration into AI Overviews).

Google DeepMind, perhaps, best exemplifies Alphabet’s leadership in AI, particularly in research. Remember that DeepMind was responsible for the AlphaFold breakthrough, which earned a Nobel Prize for its impact on protein structure prediction.

Google Cloud has grown to 12% market share in the global cloud computing market. Amazon Web Services (AMZN) and Microsoft Azure (MSFT) remain the leaders. Notably, entrenched enterprise relationships will keep Amazon and Microsoft over Google, but there is plenty of room for multiple players, and competition is good. Notably, Alphabet’s $32 billion acquisition of Wiz, which will enhance Google’s cybersecurity capabilities, helps differentiate it from its peers.

Lastly, Alphabet’s “Other Bets” segment provides optionality. For instance, Waymo, Alphabet’s autonomous vehicle company, is now “doing over 250,000 paid trips per week.” Meanwhile, Verily Health Tech Solutions looks to transform healthcare (e.g., disease detection, management, and drug discovery) by leveraging Google’s AI expertise.

Valuation Analysis and Sensitivity

Next, I conducted a discounted cash flow (DCF) analysis on GOOGL. As for inputs, I assumed 10% revenue growth and 21% FCF margins, on average, throughout the nine-year projection period. This is reasonably achievable based on GOOGL’s historical performance and market opportunities. According to a few sources, GOOGL’s beta is 1.0. This will predominantly determine its discount rate. Everything else is pretty standard for DCF methodology.

Based on inputs of 10% revenue growth and 21% FCF margins, GOOGL is slightly overvalued. Other metrics attest to this.

The following sensitivity analysis reveals how changes to growth and margin impact valuation.

So, GOOGL’s valuation assumes a growth rate closer to 11.3% (with 21% margins).

Finally, my Monte Carlo simulation accounts for input variability, providing a more realistic view of share price outcomes based on DCF model assumptions.

This tells us that GOOGL could feasibly “random walk” between $116.06 and $238.02 per share without any major changes to assumptions before becoming grossly over- or undervalued.

In my opinion, Google is likely to exceed these expectations. The company is seeing steady improvements in operating margin. This is a result of growth in high-margin segments like Google Cloud. AI unlocks a massive opportunity for Alphabet, although this will confer significant short-term investments that could keep margins from meeting their full potential.

Risks

On the flip side, Alphabet’s dependence on ads for revenue is something to watch, especially amid increasing competition from Meta (META) and Amazon. As I alluded, cloud computing and AI are fiercely competitive industries. Critically, the latter could feasibly transform the search industry, threatening a good portion of Alphabet’s market position.

What concerns me most about Alphabet is the potential for adverse regulatory outcomes. The company could be forced to divest parts of its business if viewed as a monopoly. There have been cases in the past, and you can bet your bottom dollar that this will be an ongoing threat. Still, it’s hard to see a government make significant inroads in any attempts to “break up” Alphabet, especially outside of Search. Most markets Alphabet is in (e.g., advertising, smartphones) can be categorized as “oligopolies.” This is how markets have always operated in the U.S. How much of our grocery store shelves are filled with products owned by three or four food conglomerates?

Conclusion

To wrap up, Alphabet’s first-quarter earnings results exemplify its growth opportunities. Despite heavy investments in AI, Alphabet’s ability to continually improve financial metrics says a lot. Its dominance in the global mobile OS market, search, and video platform (YouTube) is unmatched. Markets like cloud computing, AI, and autonomous vehicles are large and primed for growth, and Alphabet is strategically positioned. My reverse DCF analysis reveals that GOOGL is priced appropriately, with ample upside should the company execute.

Still, antitrust rulings and subsequent remedies could force structural changes that could hinder growth. It’s not just the U.S. DOJ targeting Alphabet; EU authorities, too, are imposing strict regulations on Google’s ability to “gatekeep” its ecosystem. Finally, Alphabet is subject to macroeconomic instability and geopolitical risks. For instance, advertising budgets are among the first expenses to be cut by businesses during times of economic hardship. Interestingly, recent AI advancements have forced Alphabet to act as both a defender (e.g., integrating AI deeply into Search and Ads) and a challenger (e.g., developing AI agents). It is all very complex.

As for a rating, its strengths and weaknesses appear fully encompassed by its valuation. However, the upside potential is too compelling to ignore. So, I am at a cautious Buy (“outperform”) on GOOGL. My takeaway opinion is that increasing competition in markets like ad tech and search is a positive development because it significantly lowers Alphabet's greatest risk (regulatory outcomes) while not having much impact on opportunity, given the massive scale of these markets.

data.title: Okta Q1 Preview: I'm Booking Profits After The 50%+ YTD Returns (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction & Investment Thesis

When I last wrote about Okta (OKTA) in March, I reiterated my “buy” rating with a price target of $136 as the company saw its cRPO (Current Remaining Performance Obligations) grow at a faster pace than the previous quarter in Q4 FY25, driven by accelerating customer count in its $100K+ and $1M+ ACV (Annual Contract Value) cohort, given its specialized go-to-market strategy, robust product innovation roadmap, and operational rigor.

Since then, the stock is up over 23%, hugely outperforming the S&P 500. Not only that, the stock is also up over 48% since my post, where I chose Okta to be my 2025 Cybersecurity pick. So far, my prediction has come true in the sense that Okta has indeed outperformed peers in the cybersecurity landscape YTD.

Okta is scheduled to report its Q1 FY26 earnings on May 27, where management has guided for revenue and non-GAAP operating income to grow 10% and 27% YoY, respectively. While beating its Q1 estimates will be positive, investors will be watching for a raise to the full-year guidance, which will be determined by cRPO, momentum in its enterprise segment, and the impact of revenue from its existing and new product lines.

As an existing investor, I believe that the forward consensus guidance for Okta is conservative, which could potentially lead to a rerating should Okta manage to sufficiently beat investor expectations, driving the stock higher. However, when I take existing growth assumptions into account, my price target shows limited upside.

With the stock having run up over 50% YTD, I have trimmed my position size for risk management purposes.

Assessing both the “good” and the “bad,” I will downgrade the stock to a “hold” at the moment with a price target of $120.

A brief recap of Q4 FY25 earnings

Okta reported its Q4 FY25 earnings in March, where it generated a revenue of $682M for the quarter, growing 13% YoY with a non-GAAP operating income of $168M, which grew 30% YoY with a margin expansion of 340 basis points to 24.6%.

During the quarter, Okta saw its total customer count grow 1.0% sequentially and 3.7% YoY to 19,650, while customers with $100K+ in ACV and $1M+ in ACV grew at a faster pace of 7% and 22% YoY to 4800 and 470 customers, respectively, underscoring the strong demand for Okta’s Workforce and Customer Identity products, along with a growing portfolio of new products that is making an impact.

Particularly, when it comes to their product roadmap, 20% of all bookings in Q4 came from new products that include Okta Identity Governance, Privileged Access, Device Access, and more, with plenty of whitespace ahead to drive deeper adoption among existing and new customers.

What I also discussed in my previous post was Okta’s two-pronged approach to its go-to-market strategies, where it leverages its internal sales team and external partner ecosystem to drive larger deal sizes. When it comes to its internal sales team, the management discussed that sales productivity in Q4 has reached a multi-year high as Okta has two separate sales teams: one that uniquely focuses on engaging with IT and security buyers' needs across their Workforce and Customer Identity solutions, while the other sales team is focused on meeting the needs of developers with their Auth0 product. Meanwhile, Okta continues to harness its partner ecosystem, where 70% of deals in Q4 were partner-influenced.

What to watch out for in Q1 FY26 earnings

Okta is scheduled to report its earnings on May 27, where the management has guided for a revenue and non-GAAP operating income growth of 10% and 27% YoY to $679M and $169M, respectively. Note that on both a sequential and a year-over-year basis, the projected growth rates for both its top and bottom lines would mark a deceleration. However, I would like to point out that on a year-over-year basis, the company’s projection of its non-GAAP operating income points to a 300 basis point expansion in margin to 25%.

For the full year FY26, the management has guided for a revenue growth of roughly 9.5% YoY to $2.855B with a non-GAAP operating income of $710M, representing a margin of 25%, up 250 basis points year-over-year.

During the course of May, Okta received a “buy” rating with a price target of $140 from Loop Capital, where analyst Yun Kim believes that Auth0 represents the most strategic asset for Okta, and with OpenAI as a customer, it is “perhaps the best positioned vendor in the identity security market.” She also pointed out that Okta not only has the largest customer base among identity security vendors, but it also trades at a sizable discount to its peer group, thus representing a “highly favorable risk-reward scenario.”

At the same time, BTIG analyst Powell also said that interest is growing in Okta’s Identity & Access Governance offering per his discussions, with Okta now having completely recovered from the October 23’ security incident.

Moving forward, I will break down particular areas within the upcoming earnings call for investors to pay attention to:

Revenue: While beating its Q1 guidance will be positive news, investors will be keenly watching as to whether the management raises their full year FY26 revenue guidance.

  1. cRPO: In Q4, Okta saw its cRPO continue to accelerate, growing 15% YoY and beating management expectations by 6%. Note that cRPO is a leading indicator for future subscription growth. For Q1, the management has guided for 12% YoY growth in cRPO. Should the management beat its cRPO guidance once more, it will be an indication that Okta is able to acquire new customers, drive deeper adoption of its solution suite to existing customers, and maintain a strong renewal rate.

  2. Enterprise Momentum: One of the reasons why Okta has been outperforming the cybersecurity complex and the S&P 500 is because of its success in gaining market share in the enterprise segment, as is evidenced by its faster-growing cohort of $100K+ and $1M+ ACV customers. While the design of its internal sales team and partner ecosystem is responsible for driving a lot of the success with the enterprise segment, along with its robust product innovation roadmap, any slowdown will not be welcomed by investors.

  3. Revenue impact from new products: In Q4, 20% of new bookings came from Okta’s new products, while Okta Identity Governance along with Okta Lifecycle Management and Okta Workflows totalled $400M in ACV in governance-related businesses. Moving forward, we would need to see growing adoption of its new products among new and existing customers, with an expanding contribution to revenue.

Profitability: Similar to revenue, investors will be looking for a beat and raise in non-GAAP operating income, which would point to expanding leverage from higher spend per customer as Okta moves upmarket, while operating with financial discipline at the same time.

Revisiting my valuation

Looking forward, I will take the management’s forward revenue and earnings guidance for FY26, followed with consensus estimates for the years after, when re-evaluating my price target.

From a revenue standpoint, the management expects to grow its FY26 revenues by 9.5%, with consensus estimates pointing to revenue growth remaining in a similar range in the years after this, which will translate to a total revenue of $3.4B. I believe that these estimates are quite conservative, and I am optimistic that Okta will easily beat these projections given its momentum with the enterprise segment that should see a growing number of $100K+ and $1M+ ACV customers in the coming years across its Workforce and Customer Identity products, along with growing adoption of its new products.

In terms of profitability, the management guided for a 25% non-GAAP operating margin for FY26, which, I believe, will expand by 100 basis points every year over the next two years. This will translate into a non-GAAP operating income of $929M in FY28, with a margin of 27%, which will be equivalent to a present value of $737M when discounted at 8%.

Assigning a price-to-earnings ratio of 28, which is roughly 1.5 times the average forward price-to-earnings multiple of the S&P 500, given the growth in earnings during this period of time, we arrive at a price target of $120, which is where the stock is currently trading at.

As an existing investor, the way I see it, it is possible that Okta will raise its forward guidance with underlying strength in its enterprise segment and product innovation, which can further re-rate the stock higher. However, under the current growth projections in place, I believe there is limited upside at the moment.

Having said that, other than the valuation showing limited upside under the current assumption, there is no other compelling reason (from a business standpoint) for me to sell my entire Okta position. However, I have trimmed my position size for risk management purposes.

My final verdict and conclusions

With Okta having outperformed the index and the overall cybersecurity landscape year-to-date, I believe it needs to sufficiently exceed investor expectations in its upcoming earnings call by raising its top and bottom-line guidance for the full year.

While that is certainly possible, the current growth assumptions suggest limited upside to my price target.

As a result, I am downgrading the stock to a “hold” while waiting for the earnings call to reassess my investment thesis.

data.title: Provident Financial Is Relatively Safe From Tariff Challenges And Offering A 5.8% Dividend Yield
data.description:
Unlike most other banks, Provident Financial Services, Inc. (PFS) is expected to ride out the current tariff-related uncertainty relatively unscathed. Firstly, the company already has a robust loan pipeline, which should lead to good loan growth this year. Secondly, the margin is stable and mostly unaffected by interest rate changes. I’m expecting the earnings to grow by 79% year-over-year to $1.88 per share in 2025. The bond market turbulence will likely lead to mark-to-market losses on PFS’ sizable securities portfolio, thereby decreasing the equity book value. The company is offering a high dividend yield of 5.8% and a potential price upside of 20.1%. Hence, I’m adopting a buy rating on Provident Financial Services.

Loan Pipelines Signal a Good Year Ahead Despite the Tariffs

While loan growth has been low in recent quarters following the acquisition of Lakeland Bancorp in May 2024, the growth is about to receive a significant boost. The management mentioned in the conference call that it now has the highest pipeline in its history, of about $2.8 billion. To put this number in perspective, $2.8 billion is 14.8% of the last reported total loan balance.

The management mentioned in the conference call that it has used questionnaires to gather intelligence on the borrowers in the pipeline. The questionnaire was intended to determine if the tariff uncertainty could erode the pipeline. The findings can be gleaned from the conference call quote below.

We have not seen any clients decay out of the pipeline as a byproduct of this. It's more about pausing in certain areas, particularly in the ABL sector, than it is absolutely shutting down from the transaction.”

It’s clear from the intelligence the management has gathered that we shouldn’t expect all prospective loans in the pipeline to get converted into loans. However, the realization of even a fraction of the pipeline could lead to material loan growth.

Provident Financial operates throughout New Jersey, eastern Pennsylvania, and Nassau and Orange County, New York. It also has some exposure to other states. Further, the loan book is well diversified among different loan types, including commercial mortgages and multifamily mortgages.

In the first month after the global “reciprocal” tariff announcement, New Jersey’s and Pennsylvania’s unemployment rates have worsened, as expected. Interestingly, New York’s unemployment rate has continued to improve.

The management mentioned in the conference call that it is expecting 3% to 5% expected loan growth. Given the exceptional size of the loan pipelines, I think the management’s target is on the prudent side. As I don’t want to be too optimistic in the year of the tariffs, I’m going to assume loan growth will be towards the higher end of the management’s guidance. I’m not going to estimate a growth rate above 5%, and certainly not as high as 14.8% (the entirety of the pipeline).

The Margin’s Independence Makes PFS a Good Investment

Due to the tariffs, the counter-tariffs, and the prospects of trade deals, it is difficult to forecast inflation and economic growth. We just don’t know what the tariff level will be by the end of the year. Further, tariffs could even be eliminated at any point because they were enacted through Executive Orders. Another executive order, by the current U.S. administration or the next U.S. administration could easily and quickly nullify the tariffs. Due to the difficulty in predicting inflation and economic growth for this year, the outlook for the interest-rate trajectory is unclear.

In the current environment of heightened uncertainty, it is good to invest in a company whose earnings aren’t too sensitive to rate changes. Provident Financial is a good investment in this case because its net interest margin has historically not moved much as interest rates have changed.

The management’s rate-sensitivity simulation model also shows that the net interest income is somewhat neutral to interest rate changes.

The management mentioned in the conference call that it is expecting the net interest margin to be in the 3.35% to 3.45% range for the remainder of 2025, up from 3.34% in 1Q-2025.

Considering the net interest margin’s history and the management’s guidance, I’m predicting the margin will remain close to 3.35% - 3.36% for the last three quarters of 2025. The average margin in 2024 was 3.26%, so the margin this year will be around 10 basis points higher.

Expecting an EPS of $1.88

The loan growth and net interest margin expansion will be the main earnings catalysts this year. Further, the provision expense will likely be lower this year because the Lakeland acquisition gave it a one-off boost last year. I’m expecting the provision expense to be higher than the historical average this year, but it most probably won’t exceed last year’s excessive level.

To forecast the non-interest expense, I’m taking the management’s guidance given in the conference call. Further, I’m assuming the non-interest income will remain rangebound near the first quarter’s level.

Overall, I’m expecting the company to report earnings of $1.88 per share in 2025, up from $1.05 last year.

Bond Market Turbulence to Decrease the Equity Book Value

Yields on the long end of the treasury yield curve have shot up over the last few weeks, as per the data on the U.S. Treasury Department’s website. The tariffs and the resultant uncertainty have spooked investors, which has led to a capital flight from the bond market. Further, the tax and spending bill seeks to significantly grow the U.S. Government’s debt over the upcoming years, as per news sources. This bill has made investors worry about the credit risk and consequently raised yields of longer-tenor treasury bonds.

Unfortunately, Provident Financial has a big securities portfolio, whose market value can drop as yields rise. The balance of Available-for-Sale (“AFS”) securities stood at $2,878.8 million, and the balance of Held-to-Maturity (“HTM”) stood at $314 million at the end of March. When interest rates surged in 2022, Provident Financial’s unrealized losses on the AFS portfolio jumped by ~ $240 million. Keeping the example of 2022 in mind, I’m anticipating unrealized losses on the AFS book to reduce equity by $50 million in 2025.

The following table shows my estimates for equity and other balance sheet line items.

Adopting a BUY Rating

Provident Financial has maintained its quarterly dividend at $0.24 per share since the last quarter of 2021. This quarterly dividend and the current market price lead to a very attractive dividend yield of 5.8%. The dividend payment seems secure to me because my earnings outlook and the current dividend suggest a payout ratio of 51%, which is easily sustainable. The payout ratio averaged 54% from 2019 to 2024, so the 2025 outlook seems to be in line with the historical trend.

To determine the fair value of Provident Financial, I’m using the historical price-to-tangible book (“P/TB”) and price-to-earnings (“P/E”) multiples.

Multiplying the average P/E multiple with the forecast earnings per share of $1.88 gives a target price of $21.2 for the end of 2025.

Multiplying the average P/TB multiple with the forecast tangible book value per share of $14.5 gives a target price of $18.9 for the end of 2025.

The average price target comes out to $20.0, which suggests a 20.1% price upside from the May 22 closing market price. Adding the dividend yield of 5.8% to the price upside gives a total expected return of 25.9%. Hence, I’m going to adopt a buy rating on Provident Financial Services.

data.title: Crescent Capital BDC: Latest Quarter A Clear Sign Of A Weakening Economy (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Introduction

With the recent GDP report showing negative growth for the first time in 3 years last month, I think this is a sign of a potential recession. Despite consumer spending remaining resilient for the better part of 3 years, I think the economy could be finally cracking.

One way I like to assess this is by analyzing the BDC sector (BIZD). As a BDC enthusiast, these play a pivotal role in both of my portfolios.

While many did well from 2022 to 2024 as a result of higher for longer interest rates, many of their portfolios are starting to show signs of wear & tear.

Other sectors are also experiencing tough times due to increased uncertainty, and BDCs could fall out of favor and become less attractive due to interest rates potentially being lower in the near future.

In this article, I discuss Crescent Capital's (CCAP) latest earnings, fundamentals, and why I'm downgrading them from a buy to a hold.

Previous Buy Rating

I last covered Crescent Capital BDC at the beginning of the year in an article titled: Share Price Finally Catching Up To Fundamentals. As stated in the title, CCAP was a fundamentally sound BDC whom I thought deserved to trade at a higher multiple.

In the chart below, you can see CCAP saw solid price appreciation from January to December of last year, up double-digits.

Since my last thesis, however, their share price performance has been a bit disappointing, down over 16% in comparison to the S&P (SP500), in the green slightly at less than 1%.

And I'll touch on later why CCAP's share price is down double-digits. But during their then Q3 earnings, Crescent Capital delivered a solid performance, beating analysts' bottom-line estimates by $0.07.

Net investment income amounted to $0.63. Total investment income also beat by a solid $2.71 million, both growing 8.5% and 7.5% respectively year-over-year.

Latest Earnings Show A Weakening Economy

Crescent Capital BDC reported their Q1 earnings earlier this month, with net investment income of $0.45 and total investment income of $42.1 million.

While their bottom line exceeded their dividend of $0.42, giving them solid coverage of 107%, both their top and bottom lines saw a sizable decline from the previous quarter and year.

TII retracted from $46.4 million, while NII declined from $0.55 the previous quarter. On an annual basis, both were down from $50.4 million and $0.63 respectively, representing declines of 16.4% and 28.6% respectively.

Part of this can be attributed to interest rates being 100 basis points lower, which impacts BDCs' overall portfolio yields. Moreover, CCAP has high exposure to floating-rate debt investments with these accounting for 97.2%.

As a result, CCAP's interest income declined year-over-year from $49.4 million in Q1'24 to $41.2 million. Another reason for this is their significant increase in non-performing loans.

In their latest quarter, CCAP added a total of 4 borrowers to non-accrual status, which is quite high for a single quarter. These accounted for 3.5% of the portfolio at cost and 1.8% at fair value, up from 1.6% at cost and 0.9% at fair value. This, along with the drop in their top and bottom lines, negatively impacted their net asset value.

NAV stood at $19.62, down from $19.98 the previous quarter and $20.28 the previous year's quarter. For comparison, peer Fidus Investment (FDUS), another small-cap BDC, performed much better. FDUS' net investment income declined as well, albeit lesser, from $0.59 to $0.54.
